title: Senior Dog Sleeping More Than Usual: Causes and When to See a Vet
meta_desc: Is your senior dog sleeping more? Learn what is normal for aging pets and when increased lethargy might indicate a health issue.
content_html:
What is it?

As dogs enter their senior years, their metabolic rate slows down and their energy levels naturally decline. It is very common for older dogs to spend more time resting or in deep sleep. This is often a normal part of the aging process as their bodies require more time to recover from daily activities. However, increased sleep can also be a subtle sign of underlying issues such as osteoarthritis, cognitive dysfunction, or metabolic changes like hypothyroidism.

Is it an emergency?

Urgency level: Low. In most cases, a gradual increase in sleeping hours is not an immediate medical emergency. If your dog is still eating, drinking, and able to go outside for bathroom breaks, you can typically wait a few days to observe their behavior before scheduling a routine veterinary appointment. However, if the lethargy is sudden, or if your dog is refusing food, acting disoriented, or seems unable to stand up, you should seek veterinary care sooner.

Why a photo or video helps triage

Providing your veterinarian with a photo or video of your dog can be incredibly helpful for triage. A video of your dog attempting to stand up or walk can show signs of joint pain or neurological weakness that may not be apparent during a static exam. Additionally, a clear photo of your dog's sleeping posture or their gum color can help a professional determine if the increased sleep is due to comfort and age or a more serious circulatory or respiratory concern.

faqs.question: How many hours a day should a senior dog sleep?
faqs.answer: Most senior dogs sleep between 14 to 18 hours a day. If they are sleeping significantly more than this or are difficult to rouse, it is worth a check-up.

faqs.question: Can joint pain make my dog sleep more?
faqs.answer: Yes, chronic pain from arthritis can make a dog more reluctant to move, which often presents as them spending more time in their bed.

faqs.question: When should I call my vet immediately?
faqs.answer: Call your vet if the increased sleep is accompanied by heavy panting, sudden weight loss, coughing, or a complete lack of interest in high-value treats.
